retrieved_docs.content: The neuroprognostication guidelines suggest the Dutch clinical prediction rule in forecasting independent ambulation at 1 year. [40] [41] Clinicians must maintain a comprehensive approach to neuroprognostication, considering multiple predictive variables rather than relying solely on isolated clinical parameters. Clear communication of prognosis with patients and caregivers should openly cover expected functional limitations, the potential for neurological recovery, and approaches to enhance the quality of life despite lasting impairments.

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Injuries -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Compare the spinal cord syndromes based on pathophysiology and clinical features. Create a clinically-guided diagnostic strategy for an individual with suspected spinal cord injury. Apply evidence-based practices when determining a personalized management plan for a patient diagnosed with a spinal cord injury. Implement interprofessional healthcare team strategies to improve care coordination and advance the management of patients with spinal cord injuries. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Injuries -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Thoracic and penetrating spinal cord injuries are significantly associated with complete injuries, and penetrating injuries exhibit notably lower rates of recovery compared to blunt trauma. [37] The degree of disability directly correlates with the injury level, with higher-level injuries resulting in more significant disability and higher complication rates. Advanced modeling using machine learning algorithms has demonstrated good predictive abilities for in-hospital mortality and discharge disposition. Still, these models currently have only fair predictive capacity for major complications and prolonged hospitalization. [38] [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Spine Trauma  -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The severity of spinal cord injury significantly influences the prognosis for recovery of function. Incomplete lesions have a better prognosis compared to complete injuries. Only 10% to 25% of patients fully recover after complete spinal cord injuries, while 64% percent show partial recovery. [14] Children who sustained spinal trauma before their growth spurt can develop scoliosis secondary to cord injury. Neurological injury has a better prognosis in children compared to adults.

ASIA D: Incomplete injury with preserved motor function below the injury level At least half of these muscles have MRC grade 3 strength. ASIA E: Normal motor and sensory examination; the neurological deficits have resolved [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Spinal Cord -- Clinical Significance -- Complete Spinal Cord Injury
retrieved_docs.content: A complete spinal cord injury leads to the loss of all motor and sensory functions below the level of injury or lesion.

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Injuries -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: SCI typically carries a guarded prognosis, with limited potential for full recovery, despite advances in medical and surgical management. The underlying cause influences the long-term prognosis of SCI, the extent of neurological damage, and the timeliness and appropriateness of medical interventions. The proportion of patients experiencing improvement by at least 1 AIS/Frankel grade (AIS/Frankel conversion rate) varies according to initial injury severity: 19% for patients initially classified as grade A, 73% for grade B, 87% for grade C, and 46% for grade D. The neurological level of injury significantly influences recovery likelihood, with lumbar injuries demonstrating the highest recovery rates, followed by cervical and thoracolumbar, and lastly, thoracic injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Injuries -- History and Physical -- The International Standards for Neurological Classification of Spinal Cord Injury exam
retrieved_docs.content: Assessment of sacral sparing involves evaluating sensory and motor function at the S4–S5 segments through deep anal pressure and voluntary anal contraction, thereby determining the completeness of the injury. Injury completeness or incompleteness is then classified using the ASIA Impairment Scale (AIS). The ISNCSCI examination is practical, requiring minimal equipment, and can be performed with the patient in a supine position, making it applicable across various clinical settings and phases of patient care. While comprehensive, ISNCSCI does not include reflex or proprioception testing and thus should be complemented by additional neurologic evaluations when clinically indicated.

retrieved_docs.content: SCIs most often occur in the context of significant trauma. Thus, a comprehensive clinical assessment for concurrent injuries is necessary at the time of presentation. Recognition of the above injury patterns can help localize the location and type of injury. A thorough clinical examination, including a precise assessment of motor and sensory function, is imperative for accurate classification. Spinal cord injuries are graded using the ASIA Impairment Scale. The grading system varies based on injury severity from letters A to E (see Image. American Spinal Injury Association [ASIA] Spinal Cord Injury Scoring Sheet).

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Cord Syndrome (Archived) -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: More severe presenting symptoms and lack of significant improvement in the first 24 hours lead to a much poorer prognosis. Two additional poor prognostic factors identified are female sex and old age. Studies have shown favorable outcomes with improved neurologic function are possible in less severe cases, and fewer patients regain their full walking ability. Functional improvements can slowly develop over several years following injury. [5] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Spinal Cord(Archived) -- Clinical Significance -- Incomplete Spinal Cord Injuries (SCIs)
retrieved_docs.content: Posterior cord syndrome [33] Very rare/least common incomplete SCI pattern of injury Pathophysiology: injury to the dorsal column Mechanism(s): Subacute combined degeneration Tabes dorsalis (ie, secondary to syphilis) Multiple sclerosis Vascular malformations (arterio-venous malformation - AVM) Malignancy (eg, compressive extramedullary tumors) Degenerative conditions (eg, spondylosis) Clinical presentation: Loss of proprioception, vibration, and deep pressure sensation below the level of injury Preserved ambulatory function Patients will complain of difficulty balancing in the dark or with their eyes closed. Classic exam consistent with a positive Romberg sign Prognosis: Preserved ambulatory function; dorsal column recovery unpredictable

retrieved_docs.title: Central Cord Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: A European multicenter study comparing 110 patients with central cord syndrome with other incomplete spinal cord injury patients revealed that central cord syndrome patients tended to have greater improvement in their ASIA Impairment Scale (AIS) grade as well as better ability to ambulate after one year. However, central cord syndrome patients had lower “self-care” scores from injury onset up to one year based on the Spinal Cord Independence Measure (SCIM), likely due to impaired hand function. [46] Patients admitted to acute inpatient rehabilitation with normal leg strength and those who experienced both upper and lower limb motor recovery had the greatest improvement in completing activities of daily living (ADL) at discharge. Patients who did not demonstrate objective neurologic recovery also made substantial functional improvements after acute inpatient rehabilitation. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2086
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of cervical spine with cord lesion is identified as a specific form of كسر في الفقرة الرقبية (cervical vertebral fracture). In the broader classification scheme, other fractures are also grouped under the category of كسر في الفقرة الرقبية, indicating that they share diagnostic or anatomical characteristics with cervical spine injuries. Moreover, كسر الحوض (pelvic fracture) is noted to co‑occur with كسر في الفقرة الرقبية, highlighting a clinical pattern in which injuries to the cervical vertebrae often accompany pelvic fractures, which is important for comprehensive trauma assessment and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2896
retrieved_docs.content: تمزق جافوي is classified as a type of إصابة الجهاز العصبي المركزي (central nervous system injury). Within this hierarchy, spinal cord injury is considered a subcategory of تمزق جافوي, indicating that spinal cord injury falls under the broader category of تمزق جافوي and, consequently, is part of the spectrum of central nervous system injuries. This relationship clarifies that spinal cord injury is a specific manifestation within the larger group of injuries represented by تمزق جافوي, which itself is recognized as a central nervous system injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2228
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of nervous system is a broad diagnostic category that encompasses a diverse group of conditions, including the entities تكزز, اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب, اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث, اعتلال عصبي عضلي, التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي, الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي, داء شاركو‑ماري‑توث, and متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية. Each of these entities is classified under Other disorders of nervous system, reflecting their shared involvement of the nervous system despite differing pathophysiological mechanisms. For example, اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب and اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث represent distinct forms of neuropathy, while اعتلال عصبي عضلي denotes a neuromuscular disorder. Inflammatory respiratory involvement is captured by التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي, whereas الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي describes a reflexive degenerative process affecting the lower limb. Dاء شاركو‑ماري‑توث refers to a specific neurodegenerative disease, and متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية characterizes a syndrome combining spinal cord injury–related motor deficits with neurogenic bladder dysfunction. Recognizing that all these conditions are grouped under Other disorders of nervous system aids clinicians in systematic evaluation, differential diagnosis, and coordinated management of patients presenting with complex neurological presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 984
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of cervical spine with cord lesion is identified as a كسر في الفقرة الرقبية, placing this specific injury within the broader category of cervical vertebral fractures. The كسر في الفقرة الرقبية is classified among other fractures, indicating that it shares common diagnostic and management considerations with a range of fracture types. It is also recognized as a subcategory of إصابة في الرقبة, linking cervical vertebral fractures to the overall spectrum of neck injuries. Clinically, كسر الحوض frequently co‑occurs with كسر في الفقرة الرقبية, highlighting the importance of evaluating patients for concurrent pelvic fractures when a cervical spine fracture is present. Understanding these hierarchical and associative relationships aids clinicians in comprehensive injury assessment, appropriate imaging, and coordinated treatment planning for patients with complex trauma involving the neck and pelvis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: The condition تمزق جافوي is closely associated with the dura mater, as the dura mater serves as the primary site where findings of تمزق جافوي are observed. In addition, تمزق جافوي functions as an antecedent or parent condition for spinal cord injury, indicating that the presence of تمزق جافوي may predispose patients to subsequent spinal cord injury. Recognizing the involvement of the dura mater in تمزق جافوي and its potential role in the development of spinal cord injury is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and management of affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2463
retrieved_docs.content: Unspecified site injury is a broad injury category that encompasses more specific conditions such as intracranial injury, indicating that injuries without a clearly defined location may include trauma to the brain. This category is primarily derived from the concept of multiple injuries (إصابات متعددة), reflecting that when several injuries occur together or the exact site is not specified, they are often grouped under a general “unspecified site” label. Recognizing that unspecified site injury can represent both intracranial trauma and a collection of multiple injuries helps clinicians adopt a comprehensive assessment approach, ensuring that potential brain involvement is not overlooked and that the overall injury burden is appropriately addressed in patient care.
